content.text: Tool use — also called function calling — is the capability that transforms Claude from a text-generation model into an active agent that can interact with the external world. By defining tools (functions, APIs, database queries, code runners) and providing them to Claude alongside a user request, developers enable Claude to call those tools during its response, receive the results, and incorporate them into a final answer. This makes Claude the intelligent coordinator of complex, multi-step workflows that span real data sources, external services, and computation.
What Is Tool Use

Tool use is a structured mechanism in the Claude API that allows Claude to request the execution of developer-defined functions during the process of answering a question. A tool is defined by a JSON schema that specifies its name, a natural-language description of what it does, and the parameters it accepts. When Claude determines that calling a tool would help it answer the question, it generates a structured tool_use block in its response containing the tool name and the appropriate arguments. The developer's application then executes the tool, and the result is returned to Claude in a follow-up message.

The key characteristic of tool use is that Claude makes autonomous decisions about whether to call a tool, which tool to call, and what arguments to provide. Claude reads the tool descriptions and reasons about how to use them in the context of the user's request. Developers do not need to specify 'call this tool with these arguments' — Claude infers all of that from the tool description and the conversation context.

Tool use supports iterative multi-turn workflows. A single user request might lead to Claude calling three or four tools in sequence, using the output of each to inform the next decision. For example, Claude might call a search tool to find relevant records, then call a calculation tool to process the numbers in those records, and then call a formatting tool to produce a structured report. Each step in this chain is autonomous — Claude decides each step based on the results so far.

Unlike prompting Claude to 'call the calculate function now', tool use is a native capability where Claude understands tools through their definitions and chooses to use them when appropriate. The quality of tool use depends heavily on how well tools are defined — clear, specific descriptions produce accurate, reliable tool calls; vague descriptions lead to incorrect usage.
Why Tool Use Matters

Without tool use, Claude is limited to reasoning over the knowledge from its training and the content of the current conversation. This is powerful but inherently static — Claude cannot access today's stock prices, query your customer database, run code to verify results, or take actions in external systems. Tool use breaks this limitation, giving Claude access to any information or capability that can be exposed through a function call.
Real-time data access: Claude can call APIs to get current information — weather, stock prices, news, exchange rates, sports scores — that post-dates its training cutoff.

Private data integration: By defining database query tools, developers let Claude access proprietary data — customer records, inventory, financial reports — without including that data in every prompt.
Code execution and verification: Claude can generate code and then call a code execution tool to run it, verify the output, and correct any errors — dramatically improving code quality.
Action-taking capability: Tools can write data as well as read it — updating CRM records, sending emails, posting messages, creating calendar events, triggering workflows.
Computation accuracy: For numerical tasks, Claude can delegate calculations to a tool rather than computing them in natural language, eliminating arithmetic errors.
Multi-step automation: Claude can orchestrate a complex workflow by chaining tool calls — gather data, process it, format it, and deliver it — all in a single conversation turn.
Structured output enforcement: Forcing Claude to use a tool with a defined schema is an effective way to guarantee structured, schema-compliant output.
Third-party service integration: Any external service with an API can be exposed to Claude as a tool, making Claude the intelligent layer over an entire ecosystem of services.
Key Features of Tool Use
JSON Schema tool definitions: Tools are defined with standard JSON Schema, giving Claude precise, machine-readable knowledge of what each tool accepts.
Autonomous tool selection: Claude decides whether to use a tool, which tool to use, and what arguments to provide without explicit instruction.
Multi-tool support: A single request can include many tool definitions; Claude selects the right one for each step of its reasoning.
Parallel tool calls: Claude can call multiple independent tools in a single response turn, reducing the number of round-trips needed for complex workflows.
tool_choice control: Developers can set tool_choice to 'auto' (Claude decides), 'any' (must use a tool), or 'none' (do not use tools) for fine-grained control.
Streaming compatibility: Tool use works with streaming responses, allowing applications to show progress as Claude reasons and generates tool calls.
Error recovery: Claude reads tool error responses and can adapt — retry with different arguments, try an alternative tool, or explain why it cannot complete the request.
Typed parameter support: Tool parameters support types including strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and objects with nested schemas.
How the Tool Use Loop Works
Step 1: Define and Submit Tools

You define tools as JSON Schema objects and include them in the tools array of your API request. Each definition has a name (a valid identifier), a description (what the tool does, when to use it, what each parameter means), and an input_schema (a JSON Schema object describing the parameters). Claude reads these definitions during processing and uses them to decide when and how to call tools.
Step 2: Claude Generates a Tool Call

If Claude decides a tool call is needed, it generates a response containing a tool_use content block. This block specifies the tool name and a JSON object with the input arguments Claude has chosen. Your application needs to parse this response to detect tool_use blocks. If Claude's response contains only text, no tool was called and you can present the text to the user.
Step 3: Execute the Tool

Your application extracts the tool name and arguments from the tool_use block and executes the corresponding function, API call, or database query. This execution happens entirely on your side — Claude never actually executes anything directly. You are responsible for the security, performance, and reliability of the tool execution. After execution, you collect the result (or error) to send back to Claude.
Step 4: Return the Result and Continue

You add a new message to the conversation with role 'user' and a content block of type 'tool_result', including the tool_use_id from Claude's tool call and the result of the execution. You then send this updated message list back to Claude. Claude reads the result and either makes another tool call, generates a final text answer, or does a combination of both. This loop continues until Claude generates a pure text response.
Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

Define your tools: Write a JSON Schema for each tool. Include a clear description of what the tool does, when it should be used, what each parameter means, and what the tool returns. This description is Claude's primary source of knowledge about your tool — invest time in making it clear and specific.

Include tools in your API request: Pass the tools array alongside your messages in the API request body. Also set tool_choice to 'auto' (default), 'any', or 'none' depending on whether you want Claude to freely decide, always call a tool, or never call a tool.

Parse Claude's response for tool_use blocks: Check the content array of Claude's response for items with type 'tool_use'. If present, extract the tool name and input arguments. If not present (only text blocks), present the response to the user and the workflow is complete.

Execute the tool with the provided arguments: Use the extracted arguments to call your function or API. Validate the arguments before executing — treat them as untrusted user input that needs validation. Handle errors gracefully and prepare an appropriate error message if execution fails.

Build the tool_result message: Construct a new user message with a content block of type 'tool_result'. Set the tool_use_id to match Claude's tool_use block ID. Set the content to the result of execution, or an error message if it failed. Append this to your conversation history.

Send the updated conversation back to Claude: Make another API call with the full updated message history, including all previous turns, Claude's tool call, and your tool result. Claude will continue from where it left off.

Repeat until Claude returns a text response: Continue the loop until Claude generates a response with no tool_use blocks. This final response is the answer to the user's original question. Present it to the user.
Practical Examples
Example 1: Real-Time Data Lookup

A financial assistant application defines tools for get_stock_price (takes a ticker symbol, returns current price and change), get_company_financials (takes a ticker, returns P/E ratio, revenue, earnings), and search_news (takes a company name, returns recent news headlines). When a user asks 'How is Apple doing today compared to its 52-week average?', Claude calls get_stock_price and get_company_financials in parallel, receives the data, computes the comparison, and delivers a comprehensive answer with specific numbers.
Example 2: Database-Backed Customer Support

A customer support chatbot defines tools for lookup_order (takes order ID, returns status and shipment details), lookup_account (takes customer email, returns account info and history), and create_ticket (takes issue details, creates a support ticket). When a user asks about their order, Claude calls lookup_order and lookup_account, synthesizes the information, and either resolves the issue or creates a ticket — without any hardcoded logic for which tool to use when.
Example 3: Code Generation with Verification

A coding assistant defines a run_code tool that executes Python snippets in a sandboxed environment. When a user asks Claude to write a function to find the median of a list, Claude writes the function, calls run_code with a set of test cases, inspects the output to confirm correctness, and then presents the verified, tested implementation. If the code fails a test, Claude analyzes the output, corrects the bug, and re-runs — all autonomously.
Example 4: Multi-Step Research Workflow

A research assistant defines tools for web_search, read_url, and summarize_document. When a user asks 'What are the main arguments against carbon taxes in recent economic literature?', Claude calls web_search to find relevant papers, calls read_url to fetch the most promising results, calls summarize_document on each, and then synthesizes a structured answer drawing on multiple sources. The entire research workflow happens in a single conversation turn.
Designing Effective Tools
Writing Clear Tool Descriptions

The description field is the most important part of a tool definition because it is what Claude uses to decide when and how to use the tool. A good description answers three questions: what does the tool do, when should Claude use it, and what does each parameter mean. Be specific about what the tool returns, what errors it might produce, and any constraints on how it can be used. Avoid vague descriptions like 'fetches data' — prefer 'Retrieves the current price and 24-hour change for a given stock ticker from the exchange. Use this when the user asks about current stock prices or market data.'
Structuring Parameter Schemas

Define parameters with specific types, descriptions, and constraints. Mark required parameters explicitly. Use enums where only specific values are valid. For optional parameters, include a description that explains when they should be provided and what the default behavior is. Well-structured schemas reduce incorrect argument generation dramatically.
Handling Errors in Tool Results

Your tool result messages should communicate errors clearly when they occur. Claude reads these errors and adjusts its approach — it might retry with different arguments, try a different tool, or explain to the user that the requested information is not available. Format error messages as structured objects with a code and message field so Claude can reason about them systematically.
Security Considerations
Always validate and sanitize tool call arguments before executing them — Claude's generated arguments should be treated as untrusted user input.
Limit tool capabilities to what is necessary for the task — do not give Claude tools it does not need for the current use case.
For tools that take destructive actions (delete, send, update), implement a confirmation step that requires explicit user approval before execution.
Log all tool calls with the arguments used, the result returned, and the user session ID for audit and debugging purposes.
Apply rate limiting to tool calls to prevent runaway automation or potential abuse.
Use the minimum necessary permissions for the credentials or API keys used in tool implementations.
Consider implementing a tool call budget — a maximum number of tool calls per conversation turn — to prevent unexpectedly long or expensive agentic loops.
Limitations and Trade-offs
Implementation complexity: Tool use requires building tool schemas, execution backends, and the multi-turn message loop — more engineering than simple completions.
Latency per round-trip: Each tool call adds a network round-trip and processing time. Deep chains of tool calls can take many seconds or minutes.
Cost increases with turns: Every message in the loop resends the full conversation history as input tokens, increasing cost with each turn.
Tool quality determines output quality: Poorly described tools lead to bad tool calls. Tool design quality is a critical factor in the reliability of tool-use applications.
No direct execution: Claude cannot execute tools directly — it can only request execution. The developer's application is always in the loop.
Tips and Best Practices
Write tool descriptions as if explaining to a skilled colleague who is unfamiliar with your system — clear, specific, with examples of when to use the tool.
Keep the number of tools per request to the minimum needed for the current task — fewer tools means better tool selection accuracy.
Implement idempotent tools wherever possible so that retrying a call does not cause unintended side effects.
Use the tool_choice 'any' setting to force tool use when you need structured output from Claude — define a tool that captures the schema you want.
Design tool error messages to be informative enough for Claude to understand what went wrong and adapt accordingly.
For complex multi-step workflows, break the problem into sub-tasks and use separate tool-calling loops for each, rather than one monolithic loop.
Monitor and analyze tool call patterns in production to find tools that are frequently misused — this reveals description quality issues to fix.
Build testing frameworks for your tool schemas using a set of representative user queries and verify that Claude calls the correct tools with correct arguments.
Frequently Asked Questions
Can Claude call tools in parallel?

Yes. Claude can generate multiple tool_use blocks in a single response, each representing an independent tool call that can be executed in parallel. This is particularly useful when you need to gather data from multiple sources simultaneously — for example, fetching the weather for three cities at once or querying three different database tables. Your application should detect when multiple tool_use blocks are present and execute them concurrently before returning all results together.
What happens if a tool call fails?

If a tool call fails, you should return a tool_result message with the error information as the content. Claude will read the error and adapt — it might retry with different arguments, try an alternative approach, or explain to the user that it cannot complete the request due to the error. The quality of Claude's error recovery depends on how informative your error messages are. Generic errors like 'Internal server error' give Claude little to work with, while specific errors like 'User ID not found in database' allow Claude to reason about the failure and potentially try an alternative.
How is tool use different from plugins or extensions?

Tool use is a first-party API capability where you define and implement the tools yourself. Plugins or extensions in various AI platforms are typically a curated marketplace of pre-built integrations. Tool use gives you complete control over what tools Claude can access and how they work — you write the execution logic. This means more implementation work but also more flexibility, security control, and customization than using pre-built plugins whose implementation you do not control.
Can Claude use tools without being told to?

With tool_choice set to 'auto' (the default), Claude autonomously decides when to call a tool based on the user's request and the available tool definitions. You do not need to instruct Claude to use tools in your prompt — Claude will use them when it determines they are needed to answer the question accurately. You can disable this autonomous behavior with tool_choice set to 'none', or force tool use with 'any'.
In-Depth Analysis

Tool Use & Agents deserves careful analysis because it touches on fundamental aspects of how Claude works and how you can most effectively leverage it. This section explores the topic from multiple angles to give you a complete understanding.

From a theoretical standpoint, understanding the underlying principles helps you predict behavior in novel situations. From a practical standpoint, knowing how to apply these principles effectively is what enables you to build reliable, high-quality systems.
Theoretical foundation: Why does this work the way it does?
Practical application: How do I apply this in real projects?
Edge cases: What unusual situations should I be aware of?
Integration: How does this interact with other Claude features?
Evolution: How has this changed and where is it going?
Implementation Patterns
There are several well-established implementation patterns for Tool Use & Agents that have emerged from real-world usage. These patterns capture best practices and help you avoid common pitfalls.

Pattern 1 — Start Simple: Begin with the most straightforward implementation. Don't add complexity until you understand the simple version completely. This pattern applies universally and prevents premature optimization.
Pattern 2 — Fail Fast: Detect errors early and surface them clearly. When something goes wrong, the earlier you catch it, the easier it is to diagnose and fix.
Pattern 3 — Iterate Based on Data: Collect metrics from real usage and use them to guide improvements. Intuition is a starting point, but data reveals the truth.
Document patterns as you discover them to build organizational knowledge
Share patterns with your team to avoid duplicate work
Test patterns thoroughly before adopting them as standard practice
Be willing to abandon patterns that don't work for your specific context
Optimization Techniques
Once you have a working implementation of Tool Use & Agents, there are several optimization techniques that can improve performance, reduce costs, or enhance the user experience.

Cost optimization is often the first priority, especially for applications with high request volumes. The key strategies include using the most efficient model for each task, minimizing context window usage, and leveraging caching where appropriate.
Performance optimization focuses on reducing latency and improving throughput. Streaming responses, parallel requests, and efficient prompt design all contribute to better performance.
Profile before optimizing — measure actual bottlenecks, not assumed ones
Optimize for the constraints that matter most to your users
Test optimizations thoroughly to ensure they don't compromise quality
Document optimizations so future maintainers understand why they exist
Monitor optimization impacts over time to ensure they continue to deliver value
Quality Assurance

Maintaining quality when working with Tool Use & Agents requires systematic testing and monitoring. Quality in AI applications is different from traditional software because outputs are probabilistic rather than deterministic.

Set up evaluation frameworks that can test your implementation against representative examples. Track quality metrics over time and investigate regressions promptly. Use both automated testing and human review for a comprehensive quality assurance program.
Define clear quality criteria before building — what does 'good' look like?
Create a test dataset that represents real-world usage patterns
Run evaluations regularly, not just during development
Track quality metrics alongside cost and performance metrics
Involve end users in quality assessment through feedback mechanisms
Practical Application Guide

Understanding the practical application of this feature in real-world scenarios is essential for making the most of it. This guide provides concrete guidance for integrating this capability into your projects effectively.

Start by identifying the specific scenarios in your application where this capability adds the most value. Build a focused proof of concept to validate your understanding, then expand gradually as you gain confidence. Track the impact on your application's quality and user experience to guide further development.
Define clear acceptance criteria before implementation to guide your development
Build incrementally and test each stage before moving to the next
Collect user feedback early and often to ensure you're solving real problems
Monitor usage patterns to identify opportunities for optimization
Document what you learn for future team members working on the same feature
Plan for graceful degradation in case the capability is unavailable or unreliable
Set up proper error handling and fallback behaviors from the start
Consider the UX implications of capability limitations when designing your system
Integration with Your Existing Stack

Integrating this Claude capability with your existing technology stack requires thoughtful planning. The integration points depend on your architecture, but there are general patterns that apply across different tech stacks.

For web applications, the integration typically involves server-side API calls that keep your API keys secure. For backend services, you might integrate directly into your data processing pipelines. Mobile applications typically communicate through a backend proxy to avoid exposing credentials.
Never expose Anthropic API keys in client-side code or version control
Use environment variables or secrets management for all credentials
Implement proper rate limiting to stay within your API tier limits
Add request logging to help debug issues and optimize usage
Consider implementing a queue for high-volume applications
Test your integration thoroughly in a staging environment before production
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive overview of this advanced capability, covering both the theoretical foundations and practical implementation guidance. Apply these insights to build more powerful and effective Claude-powered applications.
